HORRENT AFFAIRS,

Conferring Day at the Royal was a tame affair.
‘When we arrived near Earlsfort Terrace, we saw a
‘waggonette “moving on’’ before a small regiment of
police. The apostle of the new Democracy, Mr. S.—S.,
‘was conspicuousin the waggonette, and a young gentle-
man, whose appearance suggested a pocket edition of
Mr. W. B. Yeats, was haranguing the police, who looked
as solemn and walked as sadly as chief mourners behind
a hearse.

It was a rather dreary spectacle, for at the
time it was drizzling, and the whole scene, including
Stephen’s Green, in mist, wasabout as uninspiriting as
a wet rag. We went our way ;- had it beon dry, we might
have followed the waggonette on the off chance of wit-
nessing a set-to between the apostle of the New Democ-

racy and the liveried elephants of Dublin Castle. But
was too wet to expect a development like that,.and
en such a sanguinary encounter between these two
smental forces would scarcely excite our interest on

University buildings. The main hall was only about
half full at 2.30, and a gentleman was playing the
“loyal ”’ organ. We had to listen to England’s Faith-
ful organ for more than half-an-hour before the Grand
Secretary of the Free and Accepted Masons, Lord Castle-
town, took the chair, and before the Deputy-Grand
Master of the Free and Accepted Masons, Sir J. Creed
Meredith, read out some report or other. Certainly
these gentlemen of the “sign’’ were prominent at the
Conferring. Previous to the commencement, the
apostle of the New Democracy made his appearance,
but the rumour that he forced his way in over the
prostrate bodies of a score of Dublin Castle elephants
is unfounded. The Grand Secretary of the Freemasons
was allowed to make his speech without very much
interruption. Now and again there was an anxious
inquiry concerning the whereabouts of the police, and
there was also a reference to the Boston tea speech of
the Freemasons’ Grand Secretary. The thing dragged
on tamely to the end. When the Grand Secretary and
Deputy-Grand Master of the Freemasons had done
their work, the organist struck up the party tune of
the Unionists of this country. There were boos and
hisses.  We sat down and put on our hat, and Mr.
S.—S., much to our surprise, stuck close to his place
on the gallery, for we expected to see him letting him-
self down by a rope and.rushing heroically on the
organ. It was a tame, almost an orderly, business
throughout, and we were sorry we wasted over two
hours there and did not stay in our office and trust to
Green or Pink to give us an account of what took place.

Of course the action of the Senate of the Royal Uni-
versity in packing the jury—for that is what it amounts
to—at the conferring proceedings was outrageous. The
party tune of the Orangemen is inflicted on the audience,
and because some of the audience protest, and rightly
protest, against the playing of what in this country has
been turned into a virulent party tume, jury-packing
precautions are taken to exclude those who would pro-
test, and a special train (3s. 9d. return) is run by the
“ Christian ’’ Young Men’s Society from Belfast to
Dublin on the conferring day.

But this, as we often say, is not only a “loyal,”” but
a tame, country. What a volume of indignation was
behind the protest? The staff of the Freeman, or pro-
minent members of it, assist at ¢ God-Save-the-King’’
British Institute of Journalist functions; the /ndepen-
dent is the organ of Humphrey’s Dump, and the Dump
is nothing if not “loyal.””  Unfortunately—and there
is nothing but confusion to be gained from attempting
not to see the fact—there is little real, active feeling
against the playing of the Orangeman’s party tune at
Earlsfort Terrace or anywhere else. Besides, if the
University authorities prescribe “ God Save the King,”’
and attempted to carefully select the audience, they con-
ferred an honorary degree on ‘the President of the Gaelic
League, and Dr. Hyde evidently saw no reason to
refuse the honour offered by the  God-Save-the-King *’
University. If the people did not want the tune, the
tune would have to go from many places, but those who
aro not in active favour of the party tune are more or
less indifferent about the matter, and a protest becomes
farcical when led in a ‘“ moving-on ”’ waggonette by the
leader of the New Democracy. Skeffy is really too
ridiculous as a revolutionary leader. He does not look
the part, and he is an irresistible cause of laughter
rather than of throne-rocking. When no real indigna-
tion exists, and the elements of a decent row are want-
ing, where is the use of kicking up a farce?

The Freeman, as usual, makes itself ridiculous over
the Royal University matter just as it did over tho
* Sligo incident.” Ifs editor, Mr. Brayden, and its Mr.
Matty Bodkin, go to God-Save-the-King *’ dinners,
and take very good care not to kick up a row. But the
Freeman, that, in a sense, is a member of the “ God-
Save_a—the—King” Institute of Journalists, climbs the rebel
kopje as nimbly as the Freeman, the advertiser of dirty
plays, trips up the Pastoral Kopje. We read in a lead-
ing articlo on Saturday of “tho effort of the Senate to
elevate that Unionist party tune, which is called ‘God-
Save the King,’ into a charter hymn of the University.”’
And again:— But the ‘loyalism,’ one cannot call it
loyalty, that makes the patient listening to a tune that
15 as degraded a party chorus in Ireland as the ¢ Boyno
Wqber ’ a test of sentiment towards the King, is, otc.”’
Evidently the God-Save-the-King ” Freeman diner-
out must have had his tongue in his check and he writ-
ing that.

The following significant resolution was passed unani-
mously at the recent meeting of the General Council of
County Councils—* That we register our approval of the
offorts made by the Gaelic League to insure the closing
of public-houses on St. Patrick’s Day, and of the action
taken by Mr. O'Mara in introducing a Bill in Parlia-
ment to render such closing of licensed houses on the
festival of our National Apostle compulsory, and we
Tecord our conviction that the provisions of this Bill
should be placed on the statute book without delay.”

This resolution did not appear in the report of the
General Council’s proceedings in the Freeman’s Journal
—it is only fair to state, however, that we have reason
to believe that the omission was merely an oversight. One
thing that strikes us as curious, as even more than
curious, is that the resolution was passed unanimously,
though one of Mr. Edward Martyn’s principal lieu-
tenants, Mr. John Sweetman, was at the General
Council, and we presume was present whilst this reso-
lution was being passed. ~He then was an assenting
party. What! Mr. Sweetman approving of Mr.
O’Mara, a recreant occupier of the “ floor *” in his endea-
vour to get a Bill through the British Parliament—a
myth as regards Ireland which Mr. Edward Martyn’s
party do not acknowledge—bunging up Bung in Ire-
land on St. Patrick’s Day! How is this? And the
General Council (including Mr. Sweetman) record their
conviction that the provisions of this Bill should be
placed upon the statute book without delay! Mr.
Edward Martyn had better look after his unruly licu-
tenant. It is bad enough to have had another-of his
licutenants, Alderman Kelly, assisting at a testimonial
to a King’s or Queen’s Address Corporation man, without
Mr. John Sweetman applauding Mr. O’Mara’s action
on—to use the phrase that we have popularised— the
floor of the British House of Commons.”

However, this is not meant very seriously; the Mar-
tynite antics are in the region of broad farce, though
they are taken seriously by Alf Foz when Alf has his
‘tongue in his check. = We hope that Mr. O’Mara will
be encouraged by this unanimous approval of his Bill
by the General Council, and that he will do all that is
possible to get it, in the words of the resolution, “placed
upon the statute book,”” before next St. Patrick’s Day.

“The Sandymount Operatic and Dramatic Society ’’
ostensibly lives to “ foster and cultivate the art of music
“ and deportment.”’” We have italicised art, and we leave
it to the disciples of Lindley Murray to decide for them-
solves why we have done so. It also exists-for “ the pro-
duction of light operas, grand operas, dramatic sketches
‘and concerts during the season.””  On Tuesday, 28rd
Qctober, this Sandymount Society produced Zes Cloches
de Corneville.  But are we not right in saying that
‘hesides the objects we have set out the “Sandymount
‘Operatic and Dramatic Society,”” this Society presided
- over by Daniel Livingstone Ramsay, Esq., J.P., U.D.C.,
is also intended to promote the welfare and ‘prosperity
~ of Mr. Bung? Anyway, on the programme of the opera

We were present last Saturday night at the Abbey
Theatre. A contributor of ours wrote last week con-
cerning the plays, so we need not refer to them again.
It was a new sensation to find one’s self amongst a
crowded house at the Abbey Theatre. It was the first
time that we did not feel a sort of loneliness in the
place. Wo felt we were in a real theatre, and not, as
we have often felt at the Abbey, as if we were wit-
nessing an attempt to bolster up a forlorn hope. Those
present constituted a genuine audience, and not a
coterie; and the re-arrangement of the minimum price
of admission, we have no doubt, has had a psychologi-
cal as well as an economic effect on the public mind.
The public now have been asked to go to the theatre,
and they have gone, and that is a good thing both for
the public and the theatre.

‘We have to look to the “ Zrish ’* Times now for infor-
mation concerning Gaelic League matters! The fol-
lowing is from the “London Correspondence’’ of Alf
of Monday last:—

It is interesting to note that Mr. Bryce is a Gaelic scholar, hav-
ing been a pupil of the erudite Profeszor Beirne Crowe, of Queen’s
College, Galway. This news will be gratifying to Gaelic Leaguers,
especially Mr. Stephen Gwynn, and the members of the Branch
of the Five Provinces, who no doubt are aware of what Mr. Bryce
thinks of their action anent the Rev. Mr. Hannay and the Clare-
morris incident. It is no exaggeration to say that the affaire
Hannay has literally convulsed Gaeldom, and the remarkable
silence of the Branch of the Five Provinces has provoked indignant
comment.

Two to one on Alf!

Woman’s Domestic Guild, ail classes of well recom-

national ’ paper in Belfast-—it is a “ truly Catholic

Gray’s Hill, Bangor. People will tell you it is hard to
got sorvants in Treland, and yet here is a “ Nationalist’”

‘The title “ Woman’s Domestic ‘Guild ”’ has a sort of

what provisions, if amy, are made in the matter of reli-

selves in the hands of this “ Woman’s Domestic Guild,”’

Last week, our readers will remember, we pointed out
that this firm recently advertised in the London Daily
Lelegraph for an assistant for high class retail trade.”’

We have received the following explanation from
Messts. Hely for their rocent advertisement in a
London paper:—We observe the paragraph in the
current issue of your paper respecting our advertise-
ment in the Daily Telegraph. — Believing that same
might leave a wrong impression on the minds of your
readers wo hasten to reply. We are always more than
anxious when requiring additions to our staff to give
Irishmen the proference, and wo take every possible
means of procuring such, both by advertising and
inquiry through trade sources.  Unfortunately, the
supply of qualified stationers in Ireland is strictly
limited, and quite inadequate to meet the requirements
of a growing business like ours constantly needing addi-
tions to our staff. We are always on the look-out for
well-cducated, intelligent youths, who would be given
an excellent business training with us, and be taught
the stationery trade in all it branches. It is exceed-
ingly difficult to get the right boys to apply, owing to
s0 many parents preferring their sons to take up either
a Civil Service, bank, or city office clerkship. This is
exceedingly regrettable, and is one cause of so many
young men having to leave this country for foreign
lands. If a clerk is advertised for hundreds. of appli-
cations are received; if a stationer, either no applica-
tioss, or a couple from persons probably quite unsuif-
able. At the present moment we are prepared to engage
two or three youths of the type above referred to, and
we undertake to give them a thorough training in our
business, with a progressive salary from the start.’’

Perhaps some of our readers who are interested in the
stationery business may have some comments to make on
this.  If Ireland is not prepared to supply suitable
youths to assist in theretail stationery business, and if
she has to import, even such persons from England, then
there is additional reason to believe there is something
very rotten about the country. What are the Christian
Brothers doing if their schools in Dublin do not turn
out at least a few boys capable and willing to assist
behind the counter of Messrs. Hely’s shop!?

So far only one Irish manufacturing firm has joined
the soap combine. Mr. Barrington has made the
astounding statement that his firm did not get an extra
ounce of trade through the Irish Industrial Revival.
The protest on the face of it, besides being extra-
ordinary, is rather too sweeping. Mr. Barrington, re-
viewing six years’ trade, is able, on his own showing, to
calculate that he did not sell one extra ounce, and there
are several ounces in a retail pennyworth of soap, as a
consequence of the Irish Revival during that six years!

All these letters referred to the period covered by the
first year’s campaign of the LEAper. Since then the
LeADER has been pegging away, and various Industrial
Associations have sprung up in the ordinary course of
development, and yet at the end of six years Mr. Bar-

a ha’penny worth of soap as a result of the movement!

Mr. Barrington has not much faith in the fighting
powers of the Irish soap industry. He told the Inde-
pendent’s representative that the English manufacturers
could have wiped out the Irish manufacturers years ago,
1ad they so wished, with their vast capital and tre-
mendous output. Though we are not in the soap busi-
ness, we very much doubt that, to say the least, so long
as the English firms competed with each other as well
as with Ireland. Of course they might do it by forming
a Trust or Combine. And now some of them have
formed a Combine, and Mr. Barrington has joined it!
The Combine can, perhaps, if it wishes, make a special
killing price for soap in Ireland calculated to wipe the
Irish independent firms from off the industrial map.
But the people of Ireland will have something to say
to that game if it is tried on. It would be a long-
drawn-out fight that even the British Combine may
hesitate to undertake. :

We have no doubt the British Soap Combine will
make a great display at Humphrey’s Dump for the
benefit of Irish industry in general, and of the Irish
s0ap industry in particular!

The London Aonach takes place this week. It will
be held at the Horticultural Society’s Hall, Vincent
Square, Westminster, and will be the largest yet held.
The Aonach opens this day (Thursday), and will con-
tinue on Friday and Saturday. The entire exhibit of
the Dripsey Woollen Mills at the Aonach have already
been purchased by the enterprising firm, Messrs. Cor-
rigan and French, of Aldersgate St.

Messrs. M. H. Gill and Son, Ltd., are the publishers
of a new book by Miss Eleanor Hull, Author of “ Pagan
Ireland,”” “ Early Christian Ireland,”” etc. Miss Hull’s
latest book is a text-book of Irish literature, and is
particularly intended to supply the requirements of
Intermediate students, but it will, no doubt, also find
many readers among the general Irish reading public.
The volume deals with the older romance literature of
Ireland, and with the ecclesiastical, topographical and
national materials produced in Ireland up to the 16th
century, and is supplied with a full chronology of
writers up to 1500, and an appendix containing the
names and summary of contents of the great vellum
manuscripts. By the way, our London readers who
visit the donach will have an opportunity of seeing
all the recent publications issued by Messrs. Gill at the
stand of Messrs. R. and T. Washbourne.  Another
leading Irish publishing firm, Messrs. Fallon and Co.,
are making a big display of their books and other goods
at the donach also.

Arbour week, which was initiated by the Irish For-
restry Society, commenced last Monday. The Society
oarnestly desires that all sympathisers with the move-
ment for the reafforestation of Ireland should, if pos-
sible, plant some trees during this week.

We were speaking recently to a well-known man who

I.—TEA.

HE Government are at the present moment discus-
sing the details of their instalment of Home Rule,

an instalment which it has now been decided to accept,
if it be of a sufficient number of shillings in the pound.
1p peapp Lteat na mard says the old proverb. It is
not then unreasonable for an individual Irishman to
offer any suggestions that may occur to him for the
solution of the problems involved. The tangible facts
which we have to go upon are (1) that there is to be
an assembly in Dublin, having a large share of control
over the administration of the country ; (2) that *ortho-
dox Nationalists’ are to have a majority in this
assembly. This second proposition is taken from a
recent article in the 7'ribune, the inspired organ of the
Government. But in addition to these funda-
mental principles certain other ‘apocryphal particulars
have gained a sort of authority as being the flesh and
blood that will clothe this bare skeleton. It is, of
course, natural to assume that nothing more than
Gladstonian Home Rule will be conceded, and hence we
may at once set down all control of the Army and Navy
and all power of imposing Protective Tariffs as taken
away from the new assembly. And, moreover,
we may expect any scheme of devolution to accord
but scanty legislative powers. But it is further sup-
posed that the control of the judiciary and the police
will still be reserved to the Imperial Parliament, that
in fiscal matters the Imperial Parliament will retain a
power of veto; and whilst the Irish assembly, after
paying the Imperial tribute of two millions, and, pre-
sumably, providing for the expeuses of law and police,
will be able to devote the rest of the proceeds of the
taxes to any purpose it may desire, it will have no

power to apply any surplus in renrission of taxation.

Now if these last particulars are adhered to rigidly,
it is no oxaggeration to say that the fiscal provisions of
the new Bill are absolutely rotten ; for it will stereotype
the worst curse of our times, namely, the tendency to
waste public money, by making it an article of the con-
stitution. The law and police vote are, as we all know,
the greatest cause of waste in our government. They
involve, as I showed some mouths ago, a yearly waste
of about one-and-a-quarter millions of money, if we take
Scotch expenditure as a standard. That is to say, we

waste on law and police about as much as we spend on -

education. But even suppose the new measure per-
mitted us to reduce the law and police estimates to the
Scotoh figure—and that is what our members must fight
for—what should we do with the money thus saved!

We could not use the saving to reduce the tax on tea,
for instance; we must maintain taxation at its present
figure, and we could only utilise the money to increase

is any truth in the statement that our country is over-

* taxed, it must remain over-taxed for ever under the

new system. ITor, following the most influential mem-
bers of the Financial Relations Commission, I repudiate
.strongly the suggestion—the contention of England at
the present day—that you can compensate for over-taxa-
tion by over-expenditure. I have a complete disbelief
i reproductive expenditure and other benevolent mea-
sures asa means of redress for excessive taxes. Indeed, it
is only necessary to read the reports of the various Com-
missions mow sitting, to become very sceptical as to most
of these ameliorative projects. I do not object to ex-
penditure suited to our means, but if there be an atom
of truth in the statement that we are taxed beyond
them, you will benefit the labourer and small farmer
very much more by reducing the tax on tea than you
ever will by establishing coaching services to Board of
Works piers.

But the situation is even worse. Supposing John
Bull, the rich man, chooses still further to increase his
taxation and expenditure to establish old-age pensions,
or to build a larger navy, for instance, our taxes must
be increased correspondingly, even though our Imperial
contribution remain the same. When the Irish poor
are beggared by a tea-tax of a shilling in the pound, we
can only compensate them by establishing more piers,
more coaching services, and increasing our army of
public officials. We are obliged by the prin-
ciple of identical taxation to take the money from
the pockets of our poor, and ouly a driblet will ever find
its way back through the Government filter. If, then,
we want really to remedy our taxation evils, we must
put-up a strong fight to obtain power of applying sur-
plus moneys in reduction of taxation. And the best
form of such power would be power to reduce the tea-
tax. But here a great difficulty meets us. Though for
many years after the Union Ireland had a separate
system of taxation and a less tax on whisky than Eng-
land, at the present day the Liberal Party will scout
any proposal for a separate system, involving as a con-
sequence the establishment of custom-houses between Ire-
land and England. Now, hitherto the establishment of
such separate custom-houses seemed to me necessary to
effect our purpose in this respect, and it is evident how
serious an obstacle it was. But a very able revenue
official has recently shown me
customs can be avoided, and if his proposals are
workable, a great difficulty will have been solved in a
very simple way, and a great boon to Ireland will
result from it. 5

According to this scheme, the tax on tea would be
collected as at present, and would, as at present, be
always the same in England and in Ireland. Suppose
then, that, as might happen any day, the English
Chancellor of the Exchequer desired to raise the tax on
tea 2d. a pound to build a few more Dreadnoughts, and
the Irish ministry, not desiring to increase the taxes on
our poor, wished it to remain at its present figure. The
tax would be raised in both countries, but anyone taking
tea out of bond for consumption wn Ireland would re-
ceive a refund of 2d. in the pound duty. But how
gecure that it would be consumed in Ireland? Well,
first, the refund would be confined to tea taken out of
TIrish bonded warehouses, and owing fio the bulky nature

_of tea, there would be little practical temptation to
smuggle it back to England. But even so, the matter
could be put beyond all doubt by introducing, in regard
to Irish tea, a system of permits such as at present
exist for spirits, so that each consignment of tea would
‘have to be accounted for by him who received it. The
coming into operation of the scheme would, of courso,
‘be made conditional, upon a difference arising hetween

the two exchequers, of England and Treland, as to the
amount of tax to be extracted from the consumer of

5 ‘tea, The schome would be equally applicable whether

it was the English who desired to incrfzase the tax or the
Trish to redress it. -And, of course, if the tax on, say,
 spirits were increased compensation could bo made by

- reducing that on tea. The maiter may scem a trifling,

en a ludicrous one; but the tax on tea is not a trifling

b

matter to the poorest of our population, and, more-
over, it is really of immense importance, as providing
the only escape-valve in a proposed fiscal system that
may easily be submitted to a serious strain. Both the
expenditure and the taxation of England will, in all
human probability, increase. If our taxation is to
increase in the same measure, and we are only to look
for compensation by increase of expenditure, wo are
making ready the way for the worst evils of officialdom

With the tea-tax as an escape-valve, we shall have a
groat deal of fiscal liberty; but in this, as in other
matters, we shall seemingly be subject to be over-ridden
by the Imperial Parliament. Our efforts should, there-
fore, bo directed to making this an unlikely coutin-
gency. The suggestion in a former devolution scheme
that, in default of an actual statute being passed, it
should require a two-thirds majority in hoth Houses to
over-ride the Irish assembly, seems a reasonable one
which we might hope to carry. Such a prerogative, we
may be sure, would be seldom exercised, and it is not
unreasonable to demand that the exercise of the over-
riding power should be placed above party in this
manner. In a future article I shall discuss some further
problems of domestic economy in the new semi-detached
residence we think of taking on lease.

CHANEL.

e
THE REEAL IRISH MUSIC.

"I" HERE is a lot being talked about Irish music,

and one gets tired of refuting all the heresies
put forward again and again. But when a man like
“Imaal *’ comes and talks nonsense, it is necessary to
put in a word of protest. For “Imaal ”’ is a powerful
writer, and one whose word carries weight, and, con-
sequently, his expression of heterodox opinions might
lead astray the weak ones.

I am afraid “Imaal,” who usually is very careful,
has not given sufficient thought to the subject of Irish
music. ~ Why would he, for instance, speak of
“ Archzologists *’ in this connection?  Archzology has
nothing to do with it. There is not a single old pipe
left whose key notes would tell us anything about the
intervals it played. There is not a single copy left of
old Irish notation. And as to allusions to old Irish
writers, there is not a single one, that I remember,
which throws any light on the point under dispute.
Archzology, then, unfortunately, gives us no informa-
tion on this question. Again, what is the meaning of
talking of the “alleged’” traditional methods? Is it
not rather late in the day to throw any doubt on the
fact that the Irish traditional singers and players have
their special and peculiar methods? Has “Imaal”’
never heard real Irish music?

The peculiarities of traditional Irish music may be
classed under three heads: the modes, the scale, the
style.

“Tmaal ”’ speaks in an off-hand way of the ““alleged
¢ traditional  Inish mode.”” Apparently he does not
understand at all what is meant by a mode. There is
no mode of Irish music, but there are modes. Whether
these modes have any resemblance to ancient -Greek
modes, is of secondary importance. The main point is
that they are there. Thore may be some difficulty
about their classification. Some years ago I made an:
attempt to investigate this subject. But Dr. Henebry’s
recent research seems to show that the matter is a little
more complicated than I thought. Anyhow, we may,
assert, for instance, that there are Irish melodies re-
sembling modern major melodies except that they have
a minor seventh, or that there are Irish melodies
resembling modern minor melodies except that they
have a major sixth. Would “Imaal” bring these in

conformity with “the cultivated musical art of our
own day’’ by sharpening the seventh and flattening
the sixth? Surely this would be a barbarous proceed-
ing! And yet it would seem that “Imaal’’ advocates
it.

As to the scale, that is to say, the exact interval
measurement, I do not venture to offer an opinion of
my own. But anyone dealing with this question must
take count of two facts: The first is that all tradi-
tional singers and players show in thewr intonation cer-
tain deviations from the modern scale, and the second
is that men competent to judge, like Dr. Hencbry,
maintain that these differences are essential to Irish
music. These facts must give one pause. Simply to
assert, “I do say that Irish music, if it is to live and
develop, will have to be sung and played in the scales ”
“of modern music, or “the modern ear will not put up
with any but the modern scale,” is not scientific.
Probably Dr. Henebry would advise “Imaal’’ to cut
off his modern ears and to put on a pair of Irish ears
instead, if he wants to be an Irishman.

Besides these two clearly-defined characteristics of
Irish music there are a number of others which I put
together under the heading of style. A good deal has
been written about this, and still we have no clear
theoretical conception as to what constitutes the tradi-
“tional Irish style. But, after all, it does not matter
much whether we can define it. The main thing is that
it is there, and that it ought to be kept alive. “Imaal’
calls Irish music “the noblest art-heritage we have.”’
The Irish style, however, forms an integral part of this
art-heritage. et it not be left out of sight that Irish
music is a concrete thing handed down from genecration
to generation by oral tradition. The printed notation
of Irish music is not Irish music. It is merely the
approximate fixing of some of its features in a system
of writing not designed for it. To learn Irish music
from notation only, without reference to the Irish
singers and players, seems to me just as absurd as to
learn the Irish language from Irish books only, without
reference to the Irish speakers. Irish music should be
learnt in its entirety, mode, scale, and style, from those
who have it by oral tradition.

I fear some prejudice against traditional singing has
arisen from the fact that the Irish singers are, as a rule,
more or less of a failure on the concert platform. This
fact, though regrettable, must be admitted. Perhaps
the reason for it is that the Irish singers, when taken
out of their surroundings and placed before a large
audience, acquire a self-consciousness which is detri-
mental to their art. Probably, too, singing on a con-
cert platform requires some training, which, of course,
these peasant artists have not got.  Anyhow, Irish
music must be judged by what can be heard in the cabins,
of the poor Irish-speaking people. ~ There those who
wish to learn Irish music ought to go and study. It
should be remembered, too, that it is, as a rule, the
oldest women that have the finest style of singing. If
Irish singing is to be kept alive, young people ought to
try to get the sccret of the art from these. Every grave
that closes over one of these peasant singers buries under
it a part of “the noblest art-inheritage we have.”

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT—XL.

“TLord Justice Fitzgibbon’s Constructions of The
Educational Endowments (Ireland) Act.”

ORD JUSTICE FITZGIBBON, speaking of the
actions of himself and Mr. Justico O’Brien in
relation to the above Act (Rep. 1894, p. 235), says:—
“Qur failure to concur in framing a scheme leaves
things as they are; we may have failed to do good; we
can have done no harm.”

These words look very innocent Nearly twelve years
have elapsed since the Lord Justice spoke these words;
and he thinks it no harm to have deprived 6,000 chil-
dren of an educational endowment of .£9,000 or
£10;000 a year during all that time. He would then
have given £2,000 a year to the Catholic tenants for
Technical Education, so that on his own showing they
have been desprived of £24,000. But he says that his
suggested scheme of giving £2,000 a year to the Catholic
tenants was not accepted, and that he was prevented
by “a vital principle binding upon his judicial opinion’’
from signing the scheme prepared by a majority of the
Commission.

Tt is true that his scheme offering £2,000 a year to
Catholics was rejected. I have proved over and over
that there is not a particle of evidence to prove this
“vital principle binding on his judicial opinion’’—
namely, the alleged Protestant intentions of Erasmus
Smith. I shall now prove that the Educational Endow-
ment . (Ireland) Act declared, in the most express terms,
that the Erasmus Smith Endowment belongs to the
tenants’ and inhabitants childven on all his estates,
and to some others designated; and that this Act com-
manded him and his fellow-Commissioners to frame a
scheme that would enable all those children—97 per
cent. of whom are Catholics—to partake of the Endow-
ment; and that the scheme proposed, if amended by
the amendments proposed by Father Humphreys, would
have enabled allethe tenants’ children, and others desig-
nated by Erasmus Smith, to partake of the Endowment
to ‘the fullest extent. (For proof of third and last pro-
position seco Amendments (Rep., 1892).

Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, as one of the two Judicial
‘Commissioners, could block any schemo by refusing to
sign it. He used his position to block the scheme pro-
posed by refusing to sign it; and thus he has deprived
6,000 children of their birth-right to this Endowment ;
and has left it in the hands of those who have no more
claim to it than “our allies,”” the Japs. And after he
had done this, and disobeyed the law, he tells us that
‘he “has done no harm!!!”

Tn his efforts to effect his purpose he has been driven
from position to position in his construction or infer-
protation of the Educational Endowments (Ireland)
Act, and has been compelled to deliver two judicial
decisions, one of which contradicts the other.

1. The REducational Endowments (Ireland) Act
declared, in the most express terms, that the Erasmus
Smith Endowment belongs to the children of the tenants
and inhabitants on all Erasmus Smith’s estates in Ire-
land, and on all his Endowment estates, and to twenty
.other poor children within two miles of Galway, Drog-
heda and Tipperary respectively.

The 13th Section of tho Act just mentioned says:—
«Tn framing schemes it shall be the duty of the Com-
missioners, with respect alike to the constitution of the
governing hody and to educational provisions, to have
regard to the spirit of founders’ intentions, and in every
scheme which abolishes or modifies any privilege or

If tho draftsman of this section had before his mx_nd
tho claims of the children of the Erasmus Smith
tenants, and ‘ other poor children’’ entitled to th'c
Endowment, he could not have better safeguarded their

There are three clauses in Section 13 quoted above.
The first clause commands that the Commissioners are to
“ have regard to the spirit of founders’ intentions, with
respect alike to the constitution of the governing body
and to educational provisions.’

The second clause commands that when the Commis-
sioners ““abolish or modify any privileges or educa-
tional advantages to which a particular class of persons
is entitled, they are to have regard to the educational
interests of such persons.”

The third clause commands that “ where the founder
of an educational endowment has expressly provided for
the education of children belonging to the poorer
classes either generally, or of a particular class, or within
a particular area, such endowment for such
education shall continue, as far as requisite, to be
applied for the benefit of such children.”

This clause qualifies and overrules the two preceding
clauses (@) because it is placed immediately after them,
and begins with the words “Provided always;’’ (b)
because the two preceding clauses are separated from
cach other by a comma, and the third clause is separated
from the two preceding clauses by a colon, and begins

preceding directions, and is a direct and positive com-
mand that where the founder has expressly provided for
the education of children belonging to the poorer classes,
o s such endowment shall continue . . . to
be applied for the benefit of such children.”

The first clause directs the Commissioners to have
regard to the spirit of founders’ intentions with respect

tional provisions.

The second clause directs the Commissioners to have
regard to the educational interests of a particular class

ulmdify the educational advantages of that particular
class.

But in the third clause the phrase “the Commis-
sioners shall have regard *’ is dropped, and a direct and
positive command is given, that where an educational
ondowment has been provided for the poorer classes,
that endowment shall contihue to be applied for their

The shopkeepers of Lisdoonvarna do not patronise
home manufactured goods to any great extent; but
there is no lack of the comic post-cards caricaturing
Irish men and women. We took a drive on the follow-
ing Monday to Ballyvaughan by Blackhead. On the
_way the jarvey stopped at a shop in a place
called Claddagh. O’Donohue was over the door, and
we remarked that it was a good Irish name, and were
pleased, too, to see Ceuo Mile Faice in Gaelic
characters on another door. But our pleasure was of
short duration, for, on entering by that same door, the
first sight our eyes rested on was a large framed picture
illustrating Kitchener’s triumphal return from the late
Boer war; and the apartment that contained that had
not a national picture of any description. Wo hur-
riedly left the place disgusted with its shoneen owner,
and, after dniving a short distance, we halted again,
this time at a National School called Fanore.  The
Principal received us counteously, and invited us to
converse in Gaelic with his pupils, who were native Irish
speakers. The school looked bright, clean and com-

fortable, and hanging in a conspicuous place was a

card bearing the following or somewhat similar exhor-

_ tations:— Children, love your native language. Be
proud of it and never be ashamed to speak it,”” etc., etc.
The teacher, whose name I do not know, is conducting
his school on 'thoroughly Irish lines. Father Finlay’s
Readers are used by all the standards.

When we had some sight-seeing done in Bally-
vaughan, and had listened for a time to the Connemara
sailors conversing in Irish, we set about selecting a
place at which we would get tea. Having decided on
McNamara’s hotel, we entered and gave the order. We
were shown into a coffee room, and, while waiting, took
a look at the wall decorations to get some insight as to

| the character of the proprietor. What did we find in
a house which we supposed belonged to an Irishman
and situated .in an Inish-speaking village, but King
Edward VII. in coronation robes on one side of the
fireplace, and Queen Alexandra on the other. After
looking in vain for a national picture, we turned our
attention to the literature the room contained. It was
e what might be expected in the house of so loyal a
subject—the Irish Tvmes and Home Chat. y

Next day we visited Lahinch, and had our minds
made up to take tea at no shoneen house. Being com-
plete strangers, we had nothing to guide us but out-
ward appearances, so the hotel which bears Royal
Victoria in large letters on the wall was avoided, and
we entered another called “ The Marine,”” fully deter-
mined to come away, even after the order for tea was
given, if we discovered anything within pertaining to

THE PARTING OF THE WAYS.

ARY HIGGINS and William Henry O’Herlihy
were, in the speech of their little world, keep-

ing steady company.”” Both were Irish Cockneys—bub
‘with what a difference! Mary was ¢ as Irish as her own
- old grandmother at home in Connacht,” as Mrs. Hen-
nessy, with her fifty years’ experience of Irish Cockneys,
bore witness. She was born out of duo time, belonging
oss to these enervating days of indifference to race dis-

ctions than to the brave old ‘days when to be Irish
n London was to feel that the hands of most wero
' against you. The Irish Cockneys then (the term was
‘used of them and by them without hint of offence) were
jantly proud of their race, and bitterly contemptuous
' anyone who failed in love and loyalty to it. If a
ible employer asked their nationality he was told—
sh, thank God!” And the look accompanying the
told of a people who had suffered because of
“and were proud of i ‘Where bho. instincts
not naturally slavish persecution has a bracing effect.
oubt it is astringent, too; it slghtens and toughens

'THE LEADER. 173

people, and causes them to display a harsh exterior, but
decidedly it has the qualities of its defects. Thus it
was that, if the Irish in London forty years ago were
a race apart, they were, in many ways, the better for
being so. Certainly the process of absorption into the
surrounding mass of Anglo-Saxondom was slower and
more difficult then. In these facile days it is accom-
plished as smoothly, imperceptibly and inovitably as
the melting of a lump of sugar in a cup of warm tea.

Mary was not of the sort who are gracefully anxious
to grow into unity with their onvironment. She was
Irish always and in all things, except the accidents of
birth and of Cockney speech. A little aggressive, and
oven fruculent perhaps, as were her post-Famine proto-
types, she always added—" And don’t you forget it,”
when telling her nationality to any new girl in the ware-
house. It was a sore point with her that Higgins was
a doubtful sort of name. Regretfully she reflected on
the waste of her mother’s good Irish name of O’Flaherty
through absurd marriage customs. “ Mary O’Flaherty’’
would have been name and race combined, whereas
“ Mary Higgins " involved explanations, and sometimes
heated denials of the accusation of having mixed blood.
“ Thank God, there’s nothing half-and-half about me,’”’
was the way she put it.

For fourteen shillings a. week she worked ten hours a
day in a city warehouse. Upon that, being an orphan,
she was wholly dependent, yet she paid her subscription
to the Gaelic League with cheerful readiness. She could
not parse a sentence in English, and probably knew the
parts of speech as little as the signs of the zodiac, yet
she struggled smilingly through the first and second
books of O’Growney. The words and phrases, so far
from repelling, enchanted her by their very strangeness.
They belonged to the Ireland she had never seen, not
merely the words but the things they stood for. It
would have been dull learning how to ask for a tram
ticket in Irish; trams were things she had weary expe-
rience of. But to talk in Irish of twisting sugauns, or
putting sods of turf on the fire, or drawing water from
the well—all that was curiously delightful and satisfy-
ing. Tt brought her so near to the intimate home life
her dead parents had talked of with such loving fami-
liarity ; the every day life in that wonderful island of
her passionate affection, the land that was hers, and
yot not hers, the land of all beauty and all romance.
For her, at least, Parts I. and II. were full of vivid
poetry.

Because of that, however, let no one think there was
anything languishing or dreamy about Mary. She was,
in truth, alert, merry, quick-tempered, red-haired,
snub-nosed, brown-eyed, and yet—perhaps therefore—a
pretty and piquant little piece of womanhood. As Irish
as Oroagh Patrick, and in every way a thorough good
sort-—such was Mary Higgins.

And William Henry O’Herlihy?  Well, here the
sceno is changed. For one thing he was only half-Irish.
An TIrish name and an Irish—or perhaps one should say
Irish-born—father stood to his credit in that direction,
and very little more. How else could it be?! A com-
plaisant, yielding Irishman, to whom his country
speedily became “a legend emptied of concern,’” came
over and married a strong-willed woman who was Eng-
lish “all the time.””  Through his pious sister’s en-
treaties the three children of the marriage were chris-
tened in the Catholic Church—a Church to which he
rarely or never went. Sometimes in early married life
he felt vaguely that his laxity was shameful; then for
a Sunday or two he hunted the children to Mass, and
went himself. Later on he ceased to trouble about the
matter. The sister in Ireland was dead, so, too, was
his pallid apology for a conscience, and, moreover, the
children were not minded to be hunted anywhere. Thus
grew to maturity William Henry, Georgina Maud, and
Albert, Ernest O’Herlihy, three demi-semi-Catholics of
English nationality who now and again remembered
with indifference the fact of their father’s Inish birth.
They had heard the name of his village, but could not
tell you the county it was in.  “Somowhere in the

“I don’t suppose you could,”’ she retorted, “but if

She was out of the train in a twinkling, and scurry-
